figures.caption: Fig. 1 Taxonomic profile of CCGR. a Maximum likelihood phylogenetic tree of the 166 1,150 genomes of CCGR. Singleton genomes are marked with inner red dots. Color 167 range indicates the common (light grey) and unique (light purple) species compared to 168 NCBI-Refseq database. Tree branches and the first layer depict the GTDB phylum 169 annotation, the second layer describes the matching to the GTDB database at species 170 level, and the third layer shows the genome derived from pure culture (grey) and 171 contaminated culture (red). The circumferential bar plot illustrates the genome size, 172 whereas the main chromosome is shown in light green, and fragments other than main 173 chromosome are shown in dark green. b Matching of CCGR2 to the NCBI-Refseq 174 database at species-level. The Venn diagram is colored according to the origin of the 175 species and the numbers are indicated. 176

Fig. 2 Complete genomes reveal divergent strategies in genomic architecture. a 238 Distribution of genomic strand switch rates across different bacterial phyla. The 239 strand switch rate is defined as the frequency of transcriptional orientation changes 240 between adjacent genes. b Cumulative distribution function of co-directional gene 241 counts across phyla, illustrating the probability distribution of operon-like gene 242

Fig. 3 Complete genomes uncover a refined landscape of genetic elements. a The 295 raincloud plots show the difference in the number of multiple genomic metrics, 296 including base counts, CDS counts, gene counts, rRNA counts, and tRNA counts, 297 between complete genomes and the corresponding draft genomes. b The raincloud 298 plots show the difference in contig length and gene count of the phages between 299

Fig. 4 Complete genomes unveil the refined landscape of phage diversity and 354 chromosomal integration hotspots. a Improvement in phage genome assembly 355 quality. At the top is an example where sequences of CCGRVR01473 (the phage 356 predicted from a complete genome) comprised 8 different phage sequences derived 357 from a draft genome. The pie chart shows the length changes of phages from the draft 358 and phages from the complete genomes. The barplot shows the counts of different 359 numbers of phages from the draft genomes forming one phage in the complete 360 genomes. b In the innermost circle, connections between host genomes represent the 361 number of shared phages, indicating potential phage transfer events; in the second 362 circle, node colors denote the taxonomic classification of host genomes at the phylum 363 level; in the third circle, barplots illustrate the richness of phage clusters, including 364
